county_metadata.record_losses: 1863 Civil War destruction damaged courthouse; some early records lost
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Tishomingo County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Iuka. Burned county: 1863 Civil War destruction damaged the courthouse. Originally encompassed all of northeast Mississippi. Named for Chickasaw chief Tishomingo.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tishomingo County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) is a burned county due to 1863 Civil War destruction that damaged the courthouse. County seat is Iuka. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed records. The Battle of Iuka (1862) and Battle of Corinth (1862) left military records at NARA. For the burned period, use BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), MDAH microfilm, and census records. Tishomingo County originally encompassed all of northeast Mississippi before being subdivided. NARA holds Chickasaw removal records (Record Group 75). Tishomingo State Park preserves Chickasaw heritage sites. FamilySearch has digitized available Tishomingo County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the late 1830s document early settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Tishomingo County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Iuka maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses from 1863 Civil War destruction damaged courthouse; reconstructed records and surviving fragments at Chancery Clerk and MDAH

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Tunica County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Tunica. A Delta county on the Mississippi River. Once one of the poorest counties in America, transformed by casino development in the 1990s. Named for the Tunica people.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tunica County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) has Tunica as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. Tunica County is a Delta county on the Mississippi River with a historically large enslaved and African American population. MDAH has microfilm of Tunica County records. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. The county had enormous cotton plantations. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Delta are essential. The 1927 Great Flood devastated the county. Casino development beginning in the 1990s transformed the local economy. FamilySearch has digitized Tunica County clerk records. Levee board records document families along the Mississippi River. The Tunica Museum has local history collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Tunica County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Tunica maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Union County formed 1870 from Pontotoc and Tippah counties. County seat is New Albany, birthplace of William Faulkner. Located in northeast Mississippi hill country. Named to express Unionist sentiment.
county_metadata.research_tips: Union County (formed 1870 from Pontotoc and Tippah) has New Albany as county seat, birthplace of William Faulkner. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1870. For pre-1870 records, check Pontotoc County (Pontotoc) and Tippah County (Ripley). MDAH has microfilm of Union County records. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. Union County is in the hill country with a yeoman farmer economy. Freedmen's Bureau records for northeast Mississippi cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Union County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1840s-1870s document families before county formation. The Union County Heritage Museum in New Albany has local collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Union County deed books from 1870; Chancery Clerk in New Albany maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Walthall County formed 1910 from Marion and Pike counties. County seat is Tylertown. Located in southwest Mississippi. Named for General Edward C. Walthall, Confederate general and US Senator.
county_metadata.research_tips: Walthall County (formed 1910 from Marion and Pike) has Tylertown as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1910. For pre-1910 records, check Marion County (Columbia -- note 1881 fire) and Pike County (Magnolia). MDAH has microfilm of Walthall County records. Walthall County is in southwest Mississippi with a farming and timber economy. FamilySearch has some Walthall County records. Baptist church records are the primary denominational source. Tax rolls at MDAH document landholding families. Census records from 1910 capture the new county but earlier data is under Marion and Pike counties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Walthall County deed books from 1910; Chancery Clerk in Tylertown maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1862-1863 Siege of Vicksburg caused extensive Civil War damage to records; some records were preserved but others lost during the campaign
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Warren County formed 1809 from Claiborne County. County seat is Vicksburg. Burned county: 1862-1863 Siege of Vicksburg caused extensive damage. Vicksburg National Military Park preserves the battlefield. Named for General Joseph Warren of the Revolutionary War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Warren County (formed 1809 from Claiborne) is a burned county due to extensive damage during the 1862-1863 Siege of Vicksburg. County seat is Vicksburg. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed records. Vicksburg National Military Park and the Old Court House Museum hold Civil War and local history collections. MDAH has extensive microfilm of Warren County records, many filmed before wartime damage. The Old Court House Museum in Vicksburg (built 1858) is a premier local repository. Warren County had a large enslaved population, and the Siege displaced thousands. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Vicksburg sub-district are extensive. BLM GLO land patents document early entries. Catholic (St. Paul's, 1849), Baptist, Methodist, and Jewish (Anshe Chesed) congregation records are at local churches and MDAH. FamilySearch has digitized available Warren County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Warren County deed books from 1809; Chancery Clerk in Vicksburg maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses from 1862-1863 Siege of Vicksburg caused extensive Civil War damage to records; reconstructed records and surviving fragments at Chancery Clerk and MDAH

county_metadata.record_losses: 1903 courthouse fire destroyed most early records; 1927 Great Flood also caused damage
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Washington County formed 1827 from Warren and Yazoo counties. County seat is Greenville. Burned county: 1903 courthouse fire and 1927 Great Flood damage. Heart of the Mississippi Delta with vast cotton plantations. Named for President George Washington.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washington County (formed 1827 from Warren and Yazoo) is a burned county due to the 1903 courthouse fire and 1927 Great Flood damage. County seat is Greenville. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed records. MDAH has microfilm of some pre-fire Washington County records. Washington County is the heart of the Mississippi Delta with enormous cotton plantations and a predominantly African American population. Plantation records at MDAH and the Delta State University archives document enslaved communities. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Delta are essential. The 1927 Great Flood was catastrophic -- John Barry's 'Rising Tide' documents the disaster. Red Cross records at NARA document displaced families. FamilySearch has digitized available Washington County records. Greenville's literary tradition (Walker Percy, Shelby Foote) produced manuscript collections at MDAH.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Washington County deed books from 1827; Chancery Clerk in Greenville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Wayne County formed 1809 from the original vast Washington County (not the modern county). County seat is Waynesboro. Burned county: 1876 and 1882 fires destroyed early records. Located in southeast Mississippi in the Piney Woods. Named for General Anthony Wayne.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wayne County (formed 1809 from Washington County, the original vast county) is a burned county due to 1876 and 1882 fires that destroyed early records. County seat is Waynesboro. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed records. For the burned periods, use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), MDAH microfilm, and census records from 1810 forward. Wayne County is in southeast Mississippi in the Piney Woods. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. The Chickasawhay River influenced settlement patterns. FamilySearch has some Wayne County records. Baptist church records supplement the burned civil records. Tax rolls at MDAH partially fill gaps in the destroyed record base. Early settlers came from Georgia and the Carolinas.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Wayne County deed books from 1809; Chancery Clerk in Waynesboro maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Webster County formed 1874 from Chickasaw, Choctaw, Montgomery, and Oktibbeha counties. County seat is Walthall. Originally named Sumner County, renamed 1882 for Daniel Webster. Located in north-central Mississippi hill country.
county_metadata.research_tips: Webster County (formed 1874 from Chickasaw, Choctaw, Montgomery, and Oktibbeha -- originally named Sumner, renamed 1882) has Walthall as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1874. For pre-1874 records, check the four parent counties. MDAH has microfilm of Webster County records. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw and Chickasaw Cession entries. Webster County is in the hill country with a small-farm economy. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has some Webster County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records supplement civil records. Tax rolls at MDAH trace landholding families from 1874. Note the county name change from Sumner to Webster when researching early records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Webster County deed books from 1874; Chancery Clerk in Walthall maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Wilkinson County formed 1802 from Adams County. County seat is Woodville. One of Mississippi's oldest counties. Childhood home of Jefferson Davis. Located on the Louisiana border in southwest Mississippi. Named for General James Wilkinson.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wilkinson County (formed 1802 from Adams) has Woodville as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1802 and chancery court records. Wilkinson County is one of Mississippi's oldest counties with well-preserved records. MDAH has extensive microfilm of Wilkinson County land records, marriage books, chancery minutes, and estate inventories. Woodville was the childhood home of Jefferson Davis. The county had an enormous enslaved population relative to its white population. Spanish colonial records for the area are in the Mississippi Provincial Archives at MDAH. Freedmen's Bureau records for southwest Mississippi are essential. BLM GLO land patents document early entries. FamilySearch has digitized Wilkinson County chancery records. The Wilkinson County Museum has local collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Wilkinson County deed books from 1802; Chancery Clerk in Woodville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Wilkinson County Deed Books -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Wilkinson County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Wilkinson County from 1802; MDAH holds microfilm; Chancery Clerk maintains originals; Spanish and early American period land grants in the Natchez District predate federal land office records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1802; original deed books at Chancery Clerk in False

source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wilkinson County had a very large enslaved population. MDAH holds plantation records, estate inventories listing enslaved persons by name, slave schedules (1850, 1860), and slaveholder correspondence. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA (M826, M1907) document formerly enslaved families post-war

source_profiles.survival_notes: Generally good survival at MDAH

county_metadata.record_losses: 1879 courthouse fire destroyed most early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Winston County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Louisville. Burned county: 1879 courthouse fire destroyed most early records. Located in east-central Mississippi. Named for Colonel Louis Winston.
county_metadata.research_tips: Winston County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) is a burned county due to the 1879 courthouse fire that destroyed most early records. County seat is Louisville. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and post-fire records. For the burned period (1833-1879), use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), MDAH microfilm of any surviving fragments, and census records from 1840 forward. Winston County is in east-central Mississippi. The county had a significant Choctaw presence after removal -- some Choctaw families remained and are documented in NARA Record Group 75. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has some Winston County records. Baptist church records supplement the burned civil records. Tax rolls at MDAH partially fill gaps.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Winston County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Louisville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses from 1879 courthouse fire destroyed most early records; reconstructed records and surviving fragments at Chancery Clerk and MDAH
